revision.text:
'''''Mahito''''' (真人) was one of the hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] titles of ancient [[Japan]]. It was the highest in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles.
== History == Mahito was the highest in the ''Yakusa'' ''no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles (the second being ''[[Ason]]'' and the third being ''[[Sukune]]''), which was established in October 684, during the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/14970117|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|date=|others=Shōgakkan, 小学館|year=2001|isbn=4-09-526001-7|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=14970117}}</ref> ''Mahito'' was originally a Chinese [[Taoism|Taoist]] term for [[hermit]], ''shinjin'',<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/70216445|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|date=2006|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|isbn=4-09-521021-4|edition=Seisenban, shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=70216445}}</ref> but it was read as "mahito" in the ''Yakusa'' system, and was given the descendants of the [[Imperial Family]] after [[Emperor Ōjin]].<ref name=":0" />
At the beginning of the enactment, the title was given to 13 clans, after which the number was increased to 60 clans. Later, it was given to members of the Imperial Family who were demoted to nobility.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica international encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA|year=1995|location=Japan|at=真人|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
However, during the [[Nara period]], the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system was abolished, and the number of clans taking the title gradually decreased.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />
== References ==

revision.comment: +[[Category:Japanese historical terms]]; +[[Category:Titles]]; +[[Category:Ancient Japan]] using [[WP:HC|HotCat]]
revision.text:
'''''Mahito''''' (真人) was one of the hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] titles of ancient [[Japan]]. It was the highest in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles.
== History == Mahito was the highest in the ''Yakusa'' ''no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles (the second being ''[[Ason]]'' and the third being ''[[Sukune]]''), which was established in October 684, during the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/14970117|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|date=|others=Shōgakkan, 小学館|year=2001|isbn=4-09-526001-7|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=14970117}}</ref> ''Mahito'' was originally a Chinese [[Taoism|Taoist]] term for [[hermit]], ''shinjin'',<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/70216445|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|date=2006|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|isbn=4-09-521021-4|edition=Seisenban, shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=70216445}}</ref> but it was read as "mahito" in the ''Yakusa'' system, and was given the descendants of the [[Imperial Family]] after [[Emperor Ōjin]].<ref name=":0" />
At the beginning of the enactment, the title was given to 13 clans, after which the number was increased to 60 clans. Later, it was given to members of the Imperial Family who were demoted to nobility.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica international encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA|year=1995|location=Japan|at=真人|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
However, during the [[Nara period]], the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system was abolished, and the number of clans taking the title gradually decreased.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />
== References ==
[[Category:Japanese historical terms]] [[Category:Titles]] [[Category:Ancient Japan]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Noble title of ancient Japan}} '''''Mahito''''' (真人) was one of the hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] titles of ancient [[Japan]]. It was the highest in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles.
== History == Mahito was the highest in the ''Yakusa'' ''no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles (the second being ''[[Ason]]'' and the third being ''[[Sukune]]''), which was established in October 684, during the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/14970117|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|others=Shōgakkan, 小学館|year=2001|isbn=4-09-526001-7|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=14970117}}</ref> ''Mahito'' was originally a Chinese [[Taoism|Taoist]] term for [[hermit]], ''shinjin'',<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/70216445|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|date=2006|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|isbn=4-09-521021-4|edition=Seisenban, shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=70216445}}</ref> but it was read as "mahito" in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system, and was given the descendants of the [[Imperial Family]] after [[Emperor Ōjin]].<ref name=":0" />
At the beginning of the enactment, the title was given to 13 clans, after which the number was increased to 60 clans. Later, it was given to members of the Imperial Family who were demoted to nobility.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica international encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA|year=1995|location=Japan|at=真人|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
However, during the [[Nara period]], the ''kabane'' system was abolished, and the number of clans taking the title gradually decreased.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Ason]]'' * ''[[Sukune]]'' * ''[[Muraji]]''
== References == {{Reflist}}
[[Category:Japanese historical terms]] [[Category:Titles]] [[Category:Ancient Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[en:Mahito (title)]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Noble title of ancient Japan}} '''''Mahito''''' (真人) was one of the hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] titles of ancient [[Japan]]. It was the highest in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles.
== History == Mahito was the highest in the ''Yakusa'' ''no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles (the second being ''[[Ason]]'' and the third being ''[[Sukune]]''), which was established in October 684, during the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/14970117|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|others=Shōgakkan, 小学館|year=2001|isbn=4-09-526001-7|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=14970117}}</ref> ''Mahito'' was originally a Chinese [[Taoism|Taoist]] term for [[hermit]], ''shinjin'',<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/70216445|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|date=2006|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|isbn=4-09-521021-4|edition=Seisenban, shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=70216445}}</ref> but it was read as "mahito" in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system, and was given the descendants of the [[Imperial Family]] after [[Emperor Ōjin]].<ref name=":0" />
At the beginning of the enactment, the title was given to 13 clans, after which the number was increased to 60 clans. Later, it was given to members of the Imperial Family who were demoted to nobility.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica international encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA|year=1995|location=Japan|at=真人|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
However, during the [[Nara period]], the ''kabane'' system was abolished, and the number of clans taking the title gradually decreased.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Ason]]'' * ''[[Sukune]]'' * ''[[Muraji]]''
== References == {{Reflist}}

At the beginning of the enactment, the title was given to 13 clans, after which the number was increased to 60 clans. Later, it was given to members of the Imperial Family who were demoted to nobility.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica international encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA|year=1995|location=Japan|at=真人|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
However, during the [[Nara period]], the ''kabane'' system was abolished, and the number of clans taking the title gradually decreased.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Ason]]'' * ''[[Sukune]]'' * ''[[Muraji]]''

{{Short description|Noble title of ancient Japan}} '''''Mahito''''' (真人) was one of the hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] titles of ancient [[Japan]]. It was the highest in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles.
== History == Mahito was the highest in the ''Yakusa'' ''no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles (the second being ''[[Ason]]'' and the third being ''[[Sukune]]''), which was established in October 684, during the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/14970117|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|others=Shōgakkan, 小学館|year=2001|isbn=4-09-526001-7|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=14970117}}</ref> ''Mahito'' was originally a Chinese [[Taoism|Taoist]] term for [[hermit]], ''shinjin'',<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/70216445|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|date=2006|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|isbn=4-09-521021-4|edition=Seisenban, shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=70216445}}</ref> but it was read as "mahito" in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system, and was given the descendants of the [[Imperial Family]] after [[Emperor Ōjin]].<ref name=":0" />
At the beginning of the enactment, the title was given to 13 clans, after which the number was increased to 60 clans. Later, it was given to members of the Imperial Family who were demoted to nobility.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica international encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA|year=1995|location=Japan|at=真人|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
However, during the [[Nara period]], the ''kabane'' system was abolished, and the number of clans taking the title gradually decreased.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Ason]]'' * ''[[Sukune]]'' * ''[[Muraji]]''

== History == Mahito was the highest in the ''Yakusa'' ''no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles (the second being ''[[Ason]]'' and the third being ''[[Sukune]]''), which was established in October 684, during the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/14970117|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|others=Shōgakkan, 小学館|year=2001|isbn=4-09-526001-7|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=14970117}}</ref> ''Mahito'' was originally a Chinese [[Taoism|Taoist]] term for [[hermit]], ''shinjin'',<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/70216445|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|date=2006|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|isbn=4-09-521021-4|edition=Seisenban, shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=70216445}}</ref> but it was read as "mahito" in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system, and was given the descendants of the [[Imperial Family]] after [[Emperor Ōjin]].<ref name=":0" />
At the beginning of the enactment, the title was given to 13 clans, after which the number was increased to 60 clans. Later, it was given to members of the Imperial Family who were demoted to nobility.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica international encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA|year=1995|location=Japan|at=真人|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
However, during the [[Nara period]], the ''kabane'' system was abolished, and the number of clans taking the title gradually decreased.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Ason]]'' * ''[[Sukune]]'' * ''[[Muraji]]''

Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|isbn=4-09-521021-4|edition=Seisenban, shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=70216445}}</ref> but it was read as "mahito" in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system, and was given the descendants of the [[Imperial Family]] after [[Emperor Ōjin]].<ref name=":0" />

{{Short description|Noble title of ancient Japan}} '''''Mahito''''' (真人) was one of the hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] titles of ancient [[Japan]]. It was the highest in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles.
== History == Mahito was the highest in the ''Yakusa'' ''no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles (the second being ''[[Ason]]'' and the third being ''[[Sukune]]''), which was established in October 684, during the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/14970117|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|others=Shōgakkan, 小学館|year=2001|isbn=4-09-526001-7|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=14970117}}</ref> ''Mahito'' was originally a Chinese [[Taoism|Taoist]] term for [[hermit]], ''shinjin'',<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/70216445|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|date=2006|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|isbn=4-09-521021-4|edition=Seisenban, shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=70216445}}</ref> but it was read as "mahito" in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system, and was given the descendants of the [[Imperial Family]] after [[Emperor Ōjin]].<ref name=":0" />
At the beginning of the enactment, the title was given to 13 clans, after which the number was increased to 60 clans. Later, it was given to members of the Imperial Family who were demoted to nobility.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica international encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA|year=1995|location=Japan|at=真人|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
However, during the [[Nara period]], the ''kabane'' system was abolished, and the number of clans taking the title gradually decreased.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Ason]]'' * ''[[Sukune]]'' * ''[[Muraji]]''

{{Short description|Noble title of ancient Japan}} '''''Mahito''''' (真人) was one of the hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] titles of ancient [[Japan]]. It was the highest in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles.
== History == Mahito was the highest in the ''Yakusa'' ''no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles (the second being ''[[Ason]]'' and the third being ''[[Sukune]]''), which was established in October 684, during the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/14970117|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|others=Shōgakkan, 小学館|year=2001|isbn=4-09-526001-7|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=14970117}}</ref> ''Mahito'' was originally a Chinese [[Taoism|Taoist]] term for [[hermit]], ''shinjin'',<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/70216445|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|date=2006|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|isbn=4-09-521021-4|edition=Seisenban, shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=70216445}}</ref> but it was read as "mahito" in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system, and was given the descendants of the [[Imperial Family]] after [[Emperor Ōjin]].<ref name=":0" />
At the beginning of the enactment, the title was given to 13 clans, after which the number was increased to 60 clans. Later, it was given to members of the Imperial Family who were demoted to nobility.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica international encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA|year=1995|location=Japan|at=真人|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
However, during the [[Nara period]], the ''kabane'' system was abolished, and the number of clans taking the title gradually decreased.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Ason]]'' * ''[[Sukune]]'' * ''[[Muraji]]''
== References == {{Reflist}}

Later, it was given to members of the Imperial Family who were demoted to nobility.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica international encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA|year=1995|location=Japan|at=真人|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
However, during the [[Nara period]], the ''kabane'' system was abolished, and the number of clans taking the title gradually decreased.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />

{{Short description|Noble title of ancient Japan}} '''''Mahito''''' (真人) was one of the hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] titles of ancient [[Japan]]. It was the highest in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles.
== History == Mahito was the highest in the ''Yakusa'' ''no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles (the second being ''[[Ason]]'' and the third being ''[[Sukune]]''), which was established in October 684, during the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/14970117|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|others=Shōgakkan, 小学館|year=2001|isbn=4-09-526001-7|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=14970117}}</ref> ''Mahito'' was originally a Chinese [[Taoism|Taoist]] term for [[hermit]], ''shinjin'',<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/70216445|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|date=2006|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|isbn=4-09-521021-4|edition=Seisenban, shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=70216445}}</ref> but it was read as "mahito" in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system, and was given the descendants of the [[Imperial Family]] after [[Emperor Ōjin]].<ref name=":0" />
At the beginning of the enactment, the title was given to 13 clans, after which the number was increased to 60 clans. Later, it was given to members of the Imperial Family who were demoted to nobility.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica international encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA|year=1995|location=Japan|at=真人|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
However, during the [[Nara period]], the ''kabane'' system was abolished, and the number of clans taking the title gradually decreased.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />

* ''[[Ason]]'' * ''[[Sukune]]'' * ''[[Muraji]]''

revision.text:
{{Short description|Noble title of ancient Japan}} '''''Mahito''''' (真人) was one of the hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] titles of ancient [[Japan]]. It was the highest in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles.
== History == Mahito was the highest in the ''Yakusa'' ''no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles (the second being ''[[Ason]]'' and the third being ''[[Sukune]]''), which was established in October 684, during the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/14970117|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|others=Shōgakkan, 小学館|year=2001|isbn=4-09-526001-7|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=14970117}}</ref> ''Mahito'' was originally a Chinese [[Taoism|Taoist]] term for [[hermit]], ''shinjin'',<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/70216445|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|date=2006|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|isbn=4-09-521021-4|edition=Seisenban, shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=70216445}}</ref> but it was read as "mahito" in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system, and was given the descendants of the [[Imperial Family]] after [[Emperor Ōjin]].<ref name=":0" />
At the beginning of the enactment, the title was given to 13 clans, after which the number was increased to 60 clans. Later, it was given to members of the Imperial Family who were demoted to nobility.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica international encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA|year=1995|location=Japan|at=真人|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
However, during the [[Nara period]], the ''kabane'' system was abolished, and the number of clans taking the title gradually decreased.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Ason]]'' * ''[[Sukune]]'' * ''[[Muraji]]''
== References == {{Reflist}}

{{Short description|Noble title of ancient Japan}} ''''Mahito''''' (真人) was one of the hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] titles of ancient [[Japan]]. It was the highest in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles.
== History == Mahito was the highest in the ''Yakusa'' ''no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles (the second being ''[[Ason]]'' and the third being ''[[Sukune]]''), which was established in October 684, during the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/14970117|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|others=Shōgakkan, 小学館|year=2001|isbn=4-09-526001-7|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=14970117}}</ref> ''Mahito'' was originally a Chinese [[Taoism|Taoist]] term for [[hermit]], ''shinjin'',<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/70216445|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|date=2006|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|isbn=4-09-521021-4|edition=Seisenban, shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=70216445}}</ref> but it was read as "mahito" in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system, and was given the descendants of the [[Imperial Family]] after [[Emperor Ōjin]].<ref name=":0" />
At the beginning of the enactment, the title was given to 13 clans, after which the number was increased to 60 clans. Later, it was given to members of the Imperial Family who were demoted to nobility.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=FRank|title=Britannica international encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA|year=1995|location=Japan|at=真人|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
However, during the [[Nara period]], the ''kabane'' system was abolished, and the number of clans taking the title gradually decreased.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Ason]]'' * ''[[Sukune]]'' * ''[[Muraji]]''
== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.comment: Bot: remove obsolete category Pages with valid en interwikis
revision.text:
{{Short description|Noble title of ancient Japan}} ''''Mahito''''' (真人) was one of the hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] titles of ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It was the highest in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles.
== History == Mahito was the highest in the ''Yakusa'' ''no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles (the second being ''[[Ason]]'' and the third being ''[[Sukune]]''), which was established in October 684, during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/14970117|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|others=Shōgakkan, 小学館|year=2001|isbn=4-09-526001-7|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=14970117}}</ref> ''Mahito'' was originally a Chinese [[Taoism|Taoist]] term for [[:en:Hermit|Hermit]], ''shinjin'',<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/70216445|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|date=2006|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|isbn=4-09-521021-4|edition=Seisenban, shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=70216445}}</ref> but it was read as "mahito" in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system, and was given the descendants of the [[:en:Imperial Family|Imperial Family]] after [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]].<ref name=":0" />
At the beginning of the enactment, the title was given to 13 clans, after which the number was increased to 60 clans. Later, it was given to members of the Imperial Family who were demoted to nobility.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=FRank|title=Britannica international encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA|year=1995|location=Japan|at=真人|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
However, during the [[Nara period]], the ''kabane'' system was abolished, and the number of clans taking the title gradually decreased.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Ason]]'' * ''[[Sukune]]'' * ''[[Muraji]]''
== References == {{Reflist}}

However, during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], the ''kabane'' system was abolished, and the number of clans taking the title gradually decreased.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />

* ''[[Ason]]'' * ''[[Sukune]]'' * ''[[Muraji]]''

It was the highest in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles.
== History == Mahito was the highest in the ''Yakusa'' ''no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles (the second being ''[[Ason]]'' and the third being ''[[Sukune]]''), which was established in October 684, during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/14970117|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|others=Shōgakkan, 小学館|year=2001|isbn=4-09-526001-7|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=14970117}}</ref> ''Mahito'' was originally a Chinese [[Taoism|Taoist]] term for [[:en:Hermit|Hermit]], ''shinjin'',<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/70216445|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|date=2006|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|isbn=4-09-521021-4|edition=Seisenban, shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=70216445}}</ref> but it was read as "mahito" in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system, and was given the descendants of the [[:en:Imperial Family|Imperial Family]] after [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]].<ref name=":0" />
At the beginning of the enactment, the title was given to 13 clans, after which the number was increased to 60 clans. Later, it was given to members of the Imperial Family who were demoted to nobility.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=FRank|title=Britannica international encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA|year=1995|location=Japan|at=真人|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
However, during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], the ''kabane'' system was abolished, and the number of clans taking the title gradually decreased.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />

{{Short description|Noble title of ancient Japan}} ''''Mahito''''' (真人) was one of the hereditary [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble]] titles of ancient {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It was the highest in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles.
== History == Mahito was the highest in the ''Yakusa'' ''no kabane'' system of eight ''[[kabane]]'' titles (the second being ''[[Ason]]'' and the third being ''[[Sukune]]''), which was established in October 684, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/14970117|title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho|others=Shōgakkan, 小学館|year=2001|isbn=4-09-526001-7|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=14970117}}</ref> ''Mahito'' was originally a Chinese [[Taoism|Taoist]] term for {{ill|Hermit|en|Hermit|WD=Q189829|lt=Hermit}}, ''shinjin'',<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/70216445|title=Nihon kokugo daijiten|date=2006|publisher=Shōgakkan|others=Shōgakkan. Kokugo Jiten Henshūbu, 小学館. 国語辞典編集部.|isbn=4-09-521021-4|edition=Seisenban, shohan|location=Tōkyō|at=真人|oclc=70216445}}</ref> but it was read as "mahito" in the ''Yakusa no kabane'' system, and was given the descendants of the [[:en:Imperial Family|Imperial Family]] after {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}.<ref name=":0" />
At the beginning of the enactment, the title was given to 13 clans, after which the number was increased to 60 clans. Later, it was given to members of the Imperial Family who were demoted to nobility.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=FRank|title=Britannica international encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA|year=1995|location=Japan|at=真人|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
However, during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, the ''kabane'' system was abolished, and the number of clans taking the title gradually decreased.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />
== See also ==
* ''[[Ason]]'' * ''[[Sukune]]'' * ''[[Muraji]]''
